title: Slab-Built Leaf-Shaped Jewellery Dish
subtitle: A leaf silhouette dish with incised veins and a gentle curve
excerpt: Cut an air-dry clay slab to a leaf silhouette using a real leaf as the template, incise the vein pattern with a needle tool before leather-hard, then drape over a shallow bowl to set the curve. No kiln needed.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from standard air-dry slab-building and nature-template methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here as the starting point for the dish
The stage at which clay holds its shape and no longer sticks to your hands, but still contains moisture and can be carved or smoothed
Cutting or scratching lines into clay with a pointed tool to create a decorative or representational drawing

content.text: This jewellery dish uses a large, flat leaf as both a cutting template and a design reference. The
slab
is cut to match the leaf's exact silhouette, then vein lines are drawn in by
incising

with a needle tool. Draped over a shallow bowl for drying, the dish takes on a gentle natural curve. No kiln needed. The finished piece makes an elegant tray for rings, earrings, or small items on a dressing table.

materials.name: One large flat leaf (eg magnolia, fatsia, bay)

content.text: Preparation
Choose a leaf large enough to be useful as a dish: 15-25 cm long is ideal. Large, flat leaves with clear midrib and side veins give the best results. Wipe clean and allow to dry if damp.
Cover the shallow bowl or plate with cling film. Smooth out any wrinkles.
Knead the clay for 2 minutes until soft and smooth.
Rolling and cutting the leaf shape
Roll the clay on baking paper using 5 mm guide sticks until the
slab
is an even 5 mm thick and larger than your leaf in all directions.

Lay the leaf flat on the slab, vein-side up. Hold it gently in place. Use the fettling knife to cut around the leaf silhouette, following the exact outline of the leaf including any notches or lobes in the edge.
Remove the surrounding clay scraps. Do not remove the leaf itself yet.
Incising the veins
With the leaf still in place as a guide, use the needle tool to
incise

the central midrib first. Draw a continuous line from the stem end to the tip, pressing the needle tool about 1-2 mm into the clay. Use the leaf as your guide, tracing along the midrib line you can see through the leaf or feel with the needle tip.
Incise the major side veins branching off the midrib. Space them as they appear on the leaf. Use lighter pressure for the smaller secondary veins.
Remove the leaf from the clay. Check that the vein lines read clearly. Add any extra lines freehand if needed.
Draping and drying
Lift the clay leaf carefully on its baking paper and drape it incised-side up over the cling-filmed bowl or plate. Allow the leaf shape to follow the bowl curve naturally; do not press or stretch.
Leave for 4-6 hours until
leather-hard
. Then lift off the former and place right-side up on fresh baking paper to complete drying. Allow 2-3 days total.
Once fully dry, lightly sand any rough edges with fine sandpaper if needed.
Finishing
Paint with acrylics if desired. A wash of green or gold over the surface, wiped back from the incised veins, makes the lines stand out clearly.
Apply two coats of diluted PVA (1:1) over all surfaces with the mop brush. Allow 1 hour between coats. This dish is decorative; it is not waterproof for everyday wet use.
Variations
Use a very large leaf (such as rhubarb or fatsia) for a larger fruit bowl or centrepiece tray.
Instead of incising veins, press the leaf vein-down into the slab before cutting the shape, combining both impression and silhouette in one step.
Drape over a more curved form to make a deeper spoon-shaped dish.
Make a matched set of three in different leaf shapes (oak, ivy, bay) for a grouped shelf display.

items.problem: The leaf shape lost its outline details while cutting
items.cause: The leaf shifted while the fettling knife was cutting
items.fix: Hold the leaf firmly at one end while cutting the far end first. Work in short sections rather than one continuous stroke.

items.problem: Incised lines look scratchy and uneven
items.cause: The needle was pressed at varying depths
items.fix: Use a steady, consistent downward pressure and follow the vein direction in one unbroken stroke. Practice on a clay offcut first.

items.problem: The dish cracked where the leaf point is narrowest
items.fix: Cover the whole piece loosely for the first 8 hours to slow drying. Keep wall thickness at 5 mm even at the pointed tip.

items.problem: Draping left a slight wobble rather than a smooth curve
items.cause: The baking paper bunched under the clay on the former
items.fix: Smooth the baking paper before draping and ensure the cling film on the former is wrinkle-free. Light hand pressure at leather-hard stage can correct minor warps.
